E. coli Flagella Complexation — vivarium-nfsim

This report documents two experiments modeling the hierarchical assembly of the E. coli flagellum from protein monomers, using BioNetGen/NFSim wrapped as a process-bigraph process.

Biological Background

The bacterial flagellum is assembled from ~30 different proteins through seven sequential complexation reactions. The stoichiometry is taken from vivarium-chemotaxis:

#ProductSubunits
1flhDC4× flhD + 2× flhC
2flagellar motor switch26× fliG + 34× fliM + fliN
3flagellar export apparatus subunitflhA + flhB + fliO + fliP + fliQ + fliR + fliJ + 6× fliI
4flagellar export apparatusflagellar export apparatus subunit + 12× fliH
5flagellar motorflagellar motor switch + 2× fliL + flgH + motA + motB + flgB + flgC + flgF + flgG + flgI + fliF + fliE
6flagellar hook120× flgE
7flagellaflagellar export apparatus + flagellar motor + fliC + flgL + flgK + 5× fliD + flagellar hook

Modeling Approach

BioNetGen only supports bimolecular reactions. Multi-subunit assembly (up to 120 copies of FlgE for the hook) is modeled using a scaffold-with-counters strategy:

  1. Nucleation — two monomers (the scarcest chosen first) react to create a Growing_* scaffold molecule with internal state counters.
  2. Sequential growth — monomers bind one at a time, incrementing the appropriate counter.
  3. Completion — when all counters reach their targets, the scaffold converts to the finished complex.

This generates 237 reaction rules from 7 biological reactions. The BNGL model is generated programmatically by generate_flagella_bngl.py. NFSim simulates these efficiently without enumerating the species space.

Experiment 1: Standalone Complexation

Starting with enough monomers for 5 flagella, NFSim runs for 500s. Assembly proceeds hierarchically: small complexes form first, then combine into motors, hooks, and finally complete flagella.

Sub-complexes form within seconds (FlhDC) to minutes (motor switch, export apparatus). Complete flagella appear once all sub-complexes and the 120-subunit hook are ready. Stochastic timing from NFSim produces realistic single-molecule kinetics.

All 44 observables (click to expand)

Experiment 2: Continuous Production + Complexation

Two processes composed via process-bigraph and wired to shared state:

  • MonomerProduction — adds monomers every 1s at gene-expression rates (~1 flagellum worth per 100s)
  • FlagellaComplexation — runs NFSim every 50s, consuming accumulated monomers and building complexes

Between NFSim steps, sub-complex counts carry over so that higher-order assembly (motor + hook + export → flagellum) can proceed across steps.

Top: flagella accumulate roughly linearly as production feeds complexation. Middle: high-copy monomer pools show sawtooth dynamics — production accumulates them between complexation steps, which periodically consume them. Bottom: low-copy monomers (1–5 per flagellum) hover near zero, indicating they are rate-limiting.

Top: cumulative flagella production with annotated rate. Bottom: sub-complex inventory shows transient accumulation of intermediates (motor switches, export apparatus) before they are consumed by higher-order assembly.

Running the Experiments

# Generate the BNGL model
python -m vivarium_nfsim.models.generate_flagella_bngl

# Experiment 1: standalone complexation
python -m vivarium_nfsim.experiments.flagella_complexation

# Experiment 2: production + complexation composite
python -m vivarium_nfsim.experiments.flagella_with_production

# Regenerate this report
python -m vivarium_nfsim.experiments.flagella_report
